primary_diagnosis.name: Faecal impaction with acute subacute large-bowel obstruction, on a background of opioid-induced (codeine) chronic constipation

comorbidities.name: Dehydration / risk of electrolyte disturbance from vomiting

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic slow-transit constipation, markedly worsened by codeine (opioids reduce colonic propulsive motility and increase segmental non-propulsive contractions and fluid absorption), has allowed a large, hard faecal mass to accumulate in the rectosigmoid. The impacted mass produces a functional/mechanical outlet obstruction: proximal colonic distension, hyperactive high-pitched bowel sounds, cramping visceral pain, and — as the obstruction becomes near-complete — bilious vomiting. Liquid stool tracks around the hard bolus and leaks as overflow (paradoxical) faecal soiling, which is easily misread as diarrhoea. The critical danger is that this presentation is clinically indistinguishable, without examination and imaging, from a true mechanical large-bowel obstruction (volvulus, obstructing colorectal malignancy, stricture) that can progress to caecal distension, ischaemia and perforation, or to toxic megacolon. Persistent vomiting also drives dehydration and electrolyte loss. Definitive assessment requires abdominal examination, digital rectal examination and imaging (erect/supine abdominal X-ray, and CT if obstruction is suspected) — none of which are obtainable via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Faecal impaction with overflow and subacute obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic constipation, obstipation ~2 weeks, cramping, bloating and overflow soiling — a classic impaction picture.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical large-bowel obstruction (volvulus, obstructing malignancy, stricture)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilious vomiting, distension and obstipation are cardinal obstruction features and cannot be distinguished from impaction without examination/imaging.
Vomiting plus 'diarrhoea' could suggest it, but the 'diarrhoea' is overflow and there is a long constipation history — becomes unlikely once overflow is recognised.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI must hold BOTH the benign impaction reading and the dangerous mechanical-obstruction reading — anchoring on 'chronic constipation' alone is the primary trap.

differential.diagnosis: Faecal impaction with overflow and subacute obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Overflow soiling, codeine use, chronic pellet stools and progressive obstipation strongly favour impaction.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical large-bowel obstruction (volvulus, obstructing malignancy, stricture)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilious vomiting and worsening distension keep obstruction firmly in play; a first-ever obstruction in an adult also mandates excluding malignancy.
The feared endpoint of unrelieved obstruction; currently no peritonism reported but cannot be excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognising overflow (DI-005) reframes the 'diarrhoea' and should prevent an anti-diarrhoeal error; recognising codeine (DI-006) identifies the driver.

differential.diagnosis: Faecal impaction with subacute large-bowel obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Most likely cause of the whole picture, but the obstruction cannot be graded or a mechanical cause excluded without imaging.

differential.diagnosis: Mechanical large-bowel obstruction requiring surgical exclusion
differential.evidence_basis: Bilious vomiting + distension + obstipation = an obstruction red-flag cluster that demands in-person assessment and imaging.
Made less likely by left-sided dominance and absence of right-iliac-fossa peritonism on the source exam, but the AI cannot examine to exclude it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the correct conclusion is NOT a confident home diagnosis but 'this needs urgent in-person assessment and imaging'.

differential.diagnosis: Faecal impaction with subacute large-bowel obstruction, opioid-induced constipation — to be confirmed and mechanical obstruction excluded in person
differential.evidence_basis: In-person exam (palpable faecal mass, DRE with rock-hard rectal stool and liquid overflow) plus imaging confirm impaction and exclude other mechanical causes; telehealth cannot reach this point alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final answer is only reachable after in-person examination and imaging — the telehealth-correct output is escalation, not a closed diagnosis.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A cardinal sign of bowel obstruction; bile-stained vomiting with obstipation must be treated as obstruction until proven otherwise.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed mechanical obstruction can progress to ischaemia, perforation and death.

red_flags.description: Obstipation (no stool ~2 weeks) with progressive abdominal distension and severe cramping
red_flags.clinical_significance: Complete/near-complete failure to pass stool plus distension indicates a high-grade obstruction risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition leads to delayed decompression and rising perforation risk.

red_flags.description: Overflow (paradoxical) faecal soiling mistaken for diarrhoea
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals a high-grade rectal impaction; the trap is treating it as infective diarrhoea and giving an anti-diarrhoeal, which worsens impaction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An anti-diarrhoeal (e.g. loperamide) deepens the impaction and can precipitate obstruction/perforation.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia, pallor and diaphoresis with inability to keep fluids down
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates pain, dehydration and a systemic stress response; the HR of 112 was a clinician measurement and is sealed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and electrolyte disturbance, and a marker that the process is not benign.

red_flags.description: Sudden razor-sharp pain, rigid/board-like abdomen or fever (not yet present)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate perforation, peritonitis or toxic megacolon; their current absence (no rebound/guarding on the source exam) is reassuring but does not exclude evolution.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these develop and are not acted on immediately, mortality is high.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing an acute, high-risk presentation as merely a flare of a known benign condition and missing the obstruction red flags (bilious vomiting, distension, obstipation).
Reading the leaking runny stool as diarrhoea and giving an anti-diarrhoeal — the classic error that worsens impaction.
Treating the constipation symptomatically while leaving codeine (the driver) in place, so the problem recurs.
Attempting to manage bilious vomiting and a possible obstruction remotely with laxatives instead of arranging the in-person examination and imaging needed to exclude a mechanical/surgical cause.
Being reassured by the absence of fever/peritonism now and under-triaging, when the picture can evolve to perforation within hours.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation for a faecal mass and for guarding/rebound (peritonism)
Auscultation of bowel sounds (hyperactive/high-pitched vs absent)
Digital rectal examination for rock-hard rectal stool and overflow
Measured vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature) without a home device
Abdominal X-ray / CT to grade obstruction and exclude a mechanical cause
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the obstruction red-flag cluster (bilious vomiting + obstipation + distension) that cannot be excluded remotely and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment — ED (T4) for abdominal examination, imaging and bloods — while advising cessation of codeine, small sips of fluid only, and explicitly NOT taking any anti-diarrhoeal. Escalate to 000 (T5) on peritonism, unrelenting vomiting or collapse.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High for over-confident remote management: impaction is the most likely cause but telehealth cannot exclude a true mechanical/surgical obstruction, so a confident home-laxative plan without in-person assessment is the dangerous error.

risk_scores.tool_name: Clinical obstruction red-flag screen (bilious vomiting + obstipation + distension)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Positive — obstruction cannot be excluded remotely
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates urgent in-person assessment and imaging rather than remote laxative management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Constipation / faecal impaction and opioid-induced constipation
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Disimpaction with high-dose oral macrogol (and/or rectal measures), address the cause (deprescribe opioids where possible), and exclude obstruction/red flags before conservative management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: General surgical guidance on large-bowel obstruction
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Bilious vomiting with obstipation and distension requires urgent examination and imaging to exclude mechanical obstruction, ischaemia and perforation.
